article.title: Pentagon and Linux Foundation Launch OCUDU, an Open-Source RAN Stack Aimed at Doing for Wireless What Linux Did for the Internet
article.summary: The OCUDU Ecosystem Foundation debuts at MWC 2026 with AMD, Ericsson, Nokia, NVIDIA, and Verizon among 47 founding members, pledging a production-grade open-source 5G and early 6G radio access network stack on GitHub by April.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

The Linux Foundation announced the formation of the Open Centralized Unit Distributed Unit (OCUDU) Ecosystem Foundation on March 1, 2026, at Mobile World Congress in Barcelona, creating what its backers describe as the first production-grade, open-source radio access network software stack for 5G and early 6G networks. Backed by the Pentagon's FutureG office and an initial investment from the National Spectrum Consortium, the project has attracted 47 founding organizations spanning chipmakers, carriers, defense contractors, and universities, with the full codebase scheduled for publication on GitHub in April.

The initiative marks one of the most ambitious attempts to apply the open-source development model to wireless telecommunications infrastructure, a domain that has historically been dominated by a small number of proprietary vendors.

## What We Know

The OCUDU project originated from a contract awarded in September 2025 by the National Spectrum Consortium and the Pentagon's FutureG office to AI-native wireless company DeepSig and Software Radio Systems (SRS), according to the [Linux Foundation's official announcement](https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/linux-foundation-announces-ocudu-ecosystem-foundation-to-accelerate-open-source-ai-ran-innovation-302700303.html). Over the past six months, the two companies have developed a functioning, downloadable radio access network stack covering the centralized unit (CU) and distributed unit (DU) layers of Open RAN architecture.

The premier founding members of the OCUDU Ecosystem Foundation include AMD, AT&T, DeepSig, Ericsson, Nokia, NVIDIA, SoftBank, SRS, and Verizon. An additional 21 general members have joined, including Cisco, Red Hat, T-Mobile, Keysight Technologies, and Marvell. Seventeen research institutions round out the membership, among them Georgia Tech, Rice University, UC San Diego, the University of Texas at Austin, and the MITRE Corporation, as detailed in the [Linux Foundation press release](https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/linux-foundation-announces-ocudu-ecosystem-foundation-to-accelerate-open-source-ai-ran-innovation-302700303.html).

Thomas Rondeau, principal director of the Pentagon's FutureG office, has compared OCUDU's ambition to foundational open-source projects. "What Linux did for the internet and Kubernetes did for cloud, we need for wireless communications," Rondeau said, as reported by [Defense News](https://www.defensenews.com/pentagon/2024/09/13/pentagon-readies-for-6g-the-next-of-wave-of-wireless-network-tech/). In the same report, Rondeau characterized OCUDU as one of the FutureG office's top priorities, describing it as a direct outgrowth of the Defense Department's earlier 5G Challenge competition, which incentivized companies to develop open-architecture network solutions.

Paul Sutton, CEO of Software Radio Systems, positioned the project as "the Linux of RAN" -- carrier-grade, open-source software that any operator or developer can build on, according to the [Linux Foundation announcement](https://www.prnewswire.com/news-releases/linux-foundation-announces-ocudu-ecosystem-foundation-to-accelerate-open-source-ai-ran-innovation-302700303.html). Arpit Joshipura, General Manager of Networking, Edge and IoT at the Linux Foundation, said the foundation is "building an open, trusted, and secure open source platform to power the next decade of wireless innovation."

The roadmap calls for a version 1.0 release in the coming weeks, transitioning the existing srsRAN codebase to a BSD 3-Clause license under Linux Foundation governance. Subsequent releases are planned on a six-month cadence through 2028, with planned additions including massive MIMO support, non-terrestrial network capabilities, and GPU acceleration.

## Why It Matters

Telecommunications infrastructure has long operated under a model where a handful of vendors -- chiefly Ericsson, Nokia, and Huawei -- supply proprietary, vertically integrated systems to carriers worldwide. The Open RAN movement has sought to disaggregate these systems into interoperable components, but progress has been slow in part because no single, production-ready open-source RAN stack existed for developers to build on.

OCUDU aims to fill that gap. By providing a neutral, community-governed codebase that runs across multiple hardware architectures -- including Intel and AMD x86, ARM, and NVIDIA GPUs -- the project lowers the barrier to entry for new entrants while giving established vendors a common foundation for differentiation.

The Pentagon's involvement reflects dual-use strategic interests. The U.S. Department of Defense relies on commercial wireless networks for military operations and sees open-source RAN as a way to reduce dependence on any single vendor, improve security through code transparency, and ensure that American technology standards shape the global 6G ecosystem. As Rondeau told [Defense News](https://www.defensenews.com/pentagon/2024/09/13/pentagon-readies-for-6g-the-next-of-wave-of-wireless-network-tech/), "If we can define those going into it, then as we export our technologies, we're also exporting our policies and our regulations."

The military also has specific tactical applications in mind. FutureG's broader portfolio includes Integrated Sensing and Communications (ISAC), which uses wireless signals for environmental reconnaissance, and spectrum-sharing technologies critical for contested electromagnetic environments, according to [Defense News](https://www.defensenews.com/pentagon/2024/09/13/pentagon-readies-for-6g-the-next-of-wave-of-wireless-network-tech/).

## What We Don't Know

Several important questions remain unanswered. The total investment in the OCUDU project has not been publicly disclosed. While the National Spectrum Consortium and FutureG office provided initial funding, the financial commitments of the 47 founding members are unclear.

It is also uncertain how carriers will respond once the code is available. Open RAN deployments have faced criticism for underperforming proprietary systems in real-world conditions, and operators may be reluctant to stake production networks on a codebase that is only months old. Whether the six-month release cadence can deliver features like massive MIMO and non-terrestrial networks on schedule remains to be seen.

The geopolitical dimension adds complexity. China's Huawei, which is excluded from most Western 5G networks, has been investing heavily in its own 6G research. Whether OCUDU's open approach can accelerate Western 6G development fast enough to maintain a competitive edge is an open question. Advanced 6G networks are not expected to be commercially deployed until the 2030s, leaving significant time for the competitive landscape to shift.

Finally, the governance transition from a Pentagon-funded project to a Linux Foundation community project is inherently delicate. Balancing military requirements with commercial interests and academic openness will test the foundation's ability to maintain a truly neutral development environment.
